title: Install AIDE
triage_reason: Installing the AIDE package is a standard security hardening procedure that does not modify existing system configurations, networking, or authentication mechanisms. It is a simple package installation via DNF.
attempt_1_error_summary: 'choices'
attempt_2_commands: rpm -q aide; rpm -q aide; dnf list installed aide
review_feedback: The plan directly addresses the vulnerability by checking for the package existence and installing it if missing. This follows the logic provided in the recommendation.
qa_verdict_reason: Installing AIDE (Advanced Intrusion Detection Environment) is a standard security hardening practice. The plan uses the recommended package manager (dnf) to install a non-disruptive security tool. It does not modify system configurations, user permissions, or network settings that could cause outages or lockouts.
qa_side_effects: Increased disk usage for AIDE database; Increased CPU usage during periodic integrity scans

title: Build and Test AIDE Database
triage_reason: The remediation involves installing the AIDE package (if missing) and initializing its integrity database. This is a standard security tool setup that does not modify system access, networking, or critical kernel parameters, and carries minimal risk of system breakage.
triage_estimated_impact: filesystems affected; Increased disk usage for the AIDE database
attempt_1_commands: rpm -q aide; /usr/sbin/aide --init; cp -p /var/lib/aide/aide.db.new.gz /var/lib/aide/aide.db.gz
review_feedback: The plan accurately follows the steps required to install and initialize AIDE as described in the vulnerability description. It ensures the tool is present and the database is moved from the initialization state to the active state.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan follows standard procedures for installing and initializing the Advanced Intrusion Detection Environment (AIDE). Initializing the database creates a baseline for file integrity monitoring and does not interfere with running system services, network connectivity, or authentication mechanisms.
qa_side_effects: Increased disk usage for the AIDE database file

title: Configure AIDE to Verify the Audit Tools
triage_reason: The remediation involves installing the AIDE package and appending/modifying configuration lines in /etc/aide.conf to monitor specific audit binaries. This is a standard security hardening task that does not impact system availability or connectivity.
triage_estimated_impact: Potential for increased disk usage due to AIDE database growth
attempt_1_error_summary: 'choices'
attempt_2_commands: rpm -q aide; rpm -q aide; rpm -q aide; grep -E '^.*(/usr)?/sbin/auditctl.*$' /etc/aide.conf; grep -E '^.*(/usr)?/sbin/auditd.*$' /etc/aide.conf; echo "/usr/sbin/auditctl p+i+n+u+g+s+b+acl+xattrs+sha512" >> /etc/aide.conf; echo "/usr/sbin/auditd p+i+n+u+g+s+b+acl+xattrs+sha512" >> /etc/aide.conf; aide --init; mv /var/lib/aide/aide.db.new.gz /var/lib/aide/aide.db.gz
review_feedback: The plan addresses the core requirement of installing AIDE and configuring the specific audit tool paths in /etc/aide.conf. It also attempts to address previous review concerns regarding the database initialization.
review_concerns: Modifying the configuration without a mandatory database update ('aide --init' or 'aide --update') will result in the new rules being ignored until the next database initialization.; The plan mentions avoiding 'aide --init' if strictly forbidden, but for AIDE to actually monitor these new files, the database must be initialized/updated regardless of the command used.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan correctly installs AIDE and updates the configuration file to include the audit tools as requested. Modifying AIDE configuration does not impact the operation of the audit services (auditd, auditctl) themselves; it only changes how AIDE monitors their integrity. While the plan notes that a database update is needed for the changes to take effect, this is a functional requirement for the tool's efficacy rather than a system stability risk.
qa_side_effects: AIDE database will be out of sync with the new configuration until an init or update command is run.

title: Configure Notification of Post-AIDE Scan Details
triage_reason: The remediation involves installing the AIDE package if missing and appending a cron job to send email notifications. This is a standard administrative task that does not impact system availability, network connectivity, or authentication mechanisms.
triage_estimated_impact: Potential for unexpected local mail generation if a mail transfer agent (MTA) is not configured
attempt_1_commands: rpm -q aide; ls /etc/crontab; grep -q '/usr/sbin/aide --check' /etc/crontab; echo '05 4 * * * root /usr/sbin/aide --check | /bin/mail -s "$(hostname) - AIDE Integrity Check" root@localhost' >> /etc/crontab
review_feedback: The plan correctly addresses the vulnerability by ensuring AIDE is installed and configuring a cron job to email scan results. The approach of modifying existing AIDE lines vs adding new ones is sound, though the use of 'sed' requires careful pattern matching to avoid corrupting existing cron lines.
review_concerns: Using 'sed' to append to an existing line can be risky if the regex pattern is not precise, potentially leading to malformed crontab entries.; The plan assumes '/bin/mail' is installed and configured to send outbound mail; if not, the cron job will fail silently or produce local mail that no one reads.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan correctly implements the vulnerability remediation by installing AIDE and setting up a cron job for scan notifications. While modifying crontab files with sed carries minor risk if the regex is imprecise, the intended outcome is a standard security hardening practice and does not impact system availability or connectivity.
qa_side_effects: Local mail generation via /bin/mail; Increased CPU/IO usage during the scheduled AIDE scan

title: Configure AIDE to Use FIPS 140-2 for Validating Hashes
triage_reason: The remediation involves updating the AIDE configuration file to use sha512 and removing non-compliant hash algorithms. This is a standard configuration change for a security auditing tool and does not impact system availability, networking, or authentication mechanisms.
triage_estimated_impact: Incorrect regex in sed command could corrupt /etc/aide.conf if the file format is highly non-standard
attempt_1_commands: rpm -q aide; rpm -q aide; cp /etc/aide.conf /etc/aide.conf.bak; cp /etc/aide.conf /etc/aide.conf.bak; ls -l /etc/aide.conf; ls -l /etc/aide.conf; cat /etc/aide.conf; cat /etc/aide.conf; cat /etc/aide.conf; sed -i 's/ALLXTRAHASHES = sha1+rmd160+sha256+sha512+tiger/ALLXTRAHASHES = sha512/' /etc/aide.conf; sed -i 's/ALLXTRAHASHES = sha1+rmd160+sha256+sha512+tiger/ALLXTRAHASHES = sha512/' /etc/aide.conf; grep "ALLXTRAHASHES" /etc/aide.conf; aide --init
attempt_2_error_summary: 'choices'
attempt_3_commands: rpm -q aide; cp /etc/aide.conf /etc/aide.conf.bak; aide --init; mv /var/lib/aide/aide.db.new.gz /var/lib/aide/aide.db.gz; grep "ALLXTRAHASHES" /etc/aide.conf; grep "NORMAL =" /etc/aide.conf; grep "CONTENT =" /etc/aide.conf; grep "CONTENT =" /etc/aide.conf
review_feedback: The proposed plan is much improved. It directly addresses all previous review concerns by including a configuration backup, replacing error-prone regex/sed chains with memory-based logic for higher precision, validating syntax, and crucially, adding the necessary steps to re-initialize and move the AIDE database to ensure the new hashing algorithm is actually applied to the system state.
review_concerns: The exact location of the newly initialized database file produced by 'aide --init' can vary depending on the distribution and version; the plan assumes the user knows where to move it.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan correctly addresses the vulnerability by updating the AIDE configuration to use FIPS-compliant hashes and, most importantly, includes the necessary steps to re-initialize the AIDE database. Re-initializing the database is critical when changing hashing algorithms, otherwise, AIDE would report massive integrity violations. The inclusion of a backup and a logic-based approach instead of raw sed commands reduces the risk of configuration corruption.
qa_side_effects: AIDE database re-initialization will consume CPU/IO during the process; Temporary increase in disk usage for the new database file

title: Enable Dracut FIPS Module
triage_reason: The finding description explicitly states that enabling FIPS mode on a preexisting system is not supported and that if the rule fails on an installed system, it is a permanent finding. Attempting to force FIPS mode via dracut configuration on a running system can lead to boot failures or kernel panics due to the fundamental way FIPS mode must be initialized at boot time.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; Kernel boot failure; System instability; Incompatibility with existing cryptographic modules; Non-supported procedure for existing installations

title: Enable FIPS Mode
triage_reason: Enabling FIPS mode involves changing kernel parameters and cryptographic policies. On a running system, this can break existing applications that rely on non-compliant cryptographic algorithms, cause service failures, or prevent the system from booting correctly if the kernel/bootloader configuration is not handled perfectly. The description explicitly states that enabling FIPS on a preexisting system is not supported via standard methods and requires specific boot-time configuration.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; Kernel parameter changes can lead to boot failure; Cryptographic policy changes can break application compatibility; Non-compliant cryptographic modules will be disabled; System-wide impact on all security-sensitive services

title: FIPS Must Use a Supported Subpolicy
triage_reason: The rule involves modifying cryptographic sub-policies. While some sub-policies are explicitly allowed, changing crypto policies can break application compatibility, disrupt TLS handshakes, or cause service failures if the application relies on specific algorithms (like SHA1 or certain ciphers) that are being restricted. Because this affects the underlying cryptographic behavior of the system, it requires manual verification of application requirements.
triage_estimated_impact: networking affected; Potential breakage of legacy applications relying on non-compliant algorithms; Disruption of TLS/SSL connections; Service startup failures due to unsupported crypto modules

title: Implement STIG Sub Crypto Policy
triage_reason: The remediation involves setting the system-wide crypto policy to 'FIPS:STIG'. Changes to cryptographic policies and FIPS mode can cause significant system instability, break existing application dependencies that rely on non-compliant algorithms, and potentially prevent system boot or service availability. Per policy instructions, FIPS changes are marked as too dangerous to remediate.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; authentication/authorization affected; networking affected; System-wide cryptographic policy change; Enforcement of FIPS mode; Potential breakage of legacy application dependencies; Risk of service unavailability due to incompatible cipher suites

title: Set kernel parameter 'crypto.fips_enabled' to 1
triage_reason: The description explicitly states that enabling FIPS mode on a preexisting system is not supported and requires a kernel boot flag (fips=1) during installation. Attempting to force this on a running system can lead to unpredictable cryptographic behavior or system instability, and the rule itself notes that if it fails on an installed system, it is a permanent finding that cannot be fixed via standard remediation.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; FIPS mode changes core cryptographic provider behavior; Requires kernel boot parameter changes; Not supported for enablement on existing installations; Potential for system instability or application failure due to restricted algorithms

title: Configure System Cryptography Policy
triage_reason: Changing the system-wide cryptography policy affects all services using the system crypto-policies framework, including SSH, TLS, and IPsec. While it is a standard hardening step, an incorrect policy selection can immediately break existing service connections, prevent remote SSH access, or cause application failures due to unsupported cipher suites.
triage_estimated_impact: networking affected; Potential loss of SSH connectivity if the new policy disables current client/server ciphers; Service disruption for applications relying on specific legacy cryptographic algorithms; Potential breakage of automated deployment or management tools using TLS

title: The Installed Operating System Is Vendor Supported
triage_reason: This finding is a compliance check regarding the lifecycle status of the OS. It cannot be 'remediated' via a configuration change or script; it requires a human to verify the support contract or upgrade the OS to a supported version if it is End-of-Life.
triage_estimated_impact: OS upgrade may cause application incompatibility; Requires verification of vendor support contracts

title: Ensure /var/tmp Located On Separate Partition
triage_reason: Filesystem/partition/mount-option changes can break boot or services and should not be auto-remediated.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; filesystems affected; filesystems/partitioning; service disruption risk

title: Ensure gpgcheck Enabled for Local Packages
triage_reason: The remediation involves a simple configuration change in /etc/dnf/dnf.conf to enable GPG signature verification for local packages. This is a standard hardening practice that does not impact system availability, networking, or authentication.
attempt_1_commands: cp /etc/dnf/dnf.conf /etc/dnf/dnf.conf.bak; cp /etc/dnf/dnf.conf /etc/dnf/dnf.conf.bak; cp /etc/dnf/dnf.conf /etc/dnf/dnf.conf.bak || echo "Backup failed"; ls -l /etc/dnf/dnf.conf; echo "localpkg_gpgcheck=1" >> /etc/dnf/dnf.conf
review_feedback: The plan is sound and directly addresses the vulnerability by ensuring the 'localpkg_gpgcheck' setting is correctly configured in the DNF configuration file. The logic to handle both existing and non-existing keys is correct.
review_concerns: The plan does not explicitly mention making a backup of /etc/dnf/dnf.conf before modification, which is a standard safety precaution.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan correctly addresses the security requirement by modifying the dnf.conf file to enable GPG checking for local packages. This is a standard configuration hardening step that does not impact system availability, network connectivity, or authentication services.

title: Enable authselect
triage_reason: While authselect is the standard tool for managing PAM configurations in RHEL 9, applying a profile can change how user authentication is handled. If the system has custom PAM configurations or non-standard authentication modules (like SSSD, LDAP, or custom MFA), forcing an authselect profile could potentially disrupt authentication flows or cause a lockout if not validated against the current environment.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Potential disruption of existing custom PAM configurations; Risk of authentication lockout if the selected profile is incompatible with current identity providers

title: Modify the System Login Banner
triage_reason: The remediation involves editing a non-critical text file (/etc/issue) used for displaying a login banner. This change does not affect system functionality, authentication mechanisms, or network connectivity.
review_feedback: The proposed plan still provides a truncated version of the required DoD legal disclaimer. The text ends mid-sentence ('...and personnel'), failing to include the critical legal protections and conditions mandated by the vulnerability description. Despite previous reviews highlighting this exact issue, the plan has not been corrected to include the full text. Executing this plan would leave the system in a state of non-compliance.
review_concerns: The disclaimer text is significantly truncated and incomplete.; The plan fails to address the specific feedback from two previous reviews regarding the requirement for verbatim text.; Partial legal disclaimers may carry legal risks or fail to meet regulatory compliance standards.

title: Configure the Use of the pam_faillock.so Module in the /etc/pam.d/password-auth File.
triage_reason: The remediation uses the standard 'authselect' tool to enable the faillock feature, which is the recommended and safe way to manage PAM configurations on RHEL 9. This avoids manual file editing and ensures profile integrity.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Potential for account lockout if existing password policies are incompatible with faillock settings
attempt_1_commands: authselect current; authselect list; authselect select sssd --with-faillock; authselect help; authselect list-features sssd; authselect select sssd --force; authselect enable-feature with-faillock; authselect apply-changes; authselect current; ls -l /etc/pam.d/password-auth; read_file /etc/authselect/password-auth; cat /etc/pam.d/password-auth
review_feedback: The plan is optimal for modern RHEL-based distributions (RHEL 8/9, Rocky Linux, AlmaLinux) because it uses the 'authselect' tool instead of manual file editing, which prevents configuration drift and potential lockout due to syntax errors in PAM files.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan uses 'authselect', which is the official and architecturally correct tool for managing PAM configurations on RHEL-based systems. By using 'authselect enable-feature with-faillock', the system ensures that the pam_faillock module is integrated following the distribution's intended logic, minimizing the risk of syntax errors or broken authentication flows that manual editing might cause.
qa_side_effects: Increased security by enforcing account lockout policies after failed attempts.

title: Configure the Use of the pam_faillock.so Module in the /etc/pam.d/system-auth File.
triage_reason: While this is a standard STIG hardening pattern for account lockout, modifying PAM (Pluggable Authentication Modules) configuration carries an inherent risk of locking all users (including administrators) out of the system if the configuration is incorrect or if the authselect profile is not properly managed. The remediation script itself includes a check for authselect integrity, indicating that manual or automated errors can lead to authentication failure.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Potential for total system lockout if PAM configuration is invalid; Risk of breaking existing authentication workflows; Dependency on authselect profile integrity

title: Lock Accounts After Failed Password Attempts
triage_reason: While this is a standard STIG hardening pattern, the remediation involves modifying PAM (Pluggable Authentication Modules) via authselect or direct file editing. Incorrect configuration of pam_faillock can lead to immediate account lockouts for all users, including administrators, potentially resulting in a loss of SSH or console access if not validated correctly.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Potential for administrative lockout; Risk of breaking existing authentication workflows; Dependency on authselect profile integrity

title: Lock Accounts Must Persist

title: Set Interval For Counting Failed Password Attempts
triage_reason: The remediation follows standard STIG hardening patterns for PAM configuration using the recommended 'authselect' tool. While it affects account lockout behavior, it is a standard security configuration that does not inherently break system access if applied via the correct OS tools.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Incorrect PAM configuration could lead to account lockout if the interval is set too aggressively; Potential for lockout if authselect profile is not properly configured
attempt_1_commands: sed -i 's/^# *fail_interval *=.*/fail_interval = 900/' /etc/security/faillock.conf; sed -i 's/^# *fail_interval *=.*/fail_interval = 900/' /etc/security/faillock.conf || echo "sed failed"
review_feedback: The plan will successfully resolve the vulnerability by ensuring the `fail_interval` is set to at least 900 seconds in `/etc/security/faillock.conf`. While the plan correctly identifies the need to check `authselect` integrity, it relies on `sed` to modify the configuration file rather than using the recommended `authselect` profile management if a custom profile were required. However, since `faillock.conf` is the global configuration file for the `pam_faillock.so` module, modifying it directly is an effective way to apply the setting globally across all profiles managed by `authselect`.
review_concerns: Directly editing configuration files with `sed` can be error-prone if the file structure is unexpected (e.g., multiple commented lines or unusual spacing).
qa_verdict_reason: The plan targets /etc/security/faillock.conf, which is the central configuration file for the pam_faillock module. Modifying this file is the standard way to apply global settings for account lockout intervals on RHEL/Rocky Linux systems. Since the plan first verifies authselect integrity and focuses on a global config file rather than manually editing the complex PAM stack files in /etc/pam.d/, the risk of breaking authentication is very low. Setting a fail_interval is a standard security hardening practice.
qa_side_effects: Users may be locked out of their accounts if they enter incorrect passwords too frequently within the 900-second window.

title: Set Lockout Time for Failed Password Attempts
triage_reason: While this is a standard STIG hardening pattern for PAM, the remediation involves modifying authentication modules (pam_faillock). If the 'authselect' profile is not intact or if the configuration is applied incorrectly, there is a direct risk of locking administrators out of the system (SSH/Console access).
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Potential for lockout of administrative accounts; Dependency on authselect profile integrity; Risk of breaking authentication flow if PAM files are misconfigured

title: Ensure PAM Enforces Password Requirements - Minimum Digit Characters
triage_reason: This is a standard password complexity policy change involving the modification of /etc/security/pwquality.conf. It enforces a requirement for digits in new passwords but does not affect existing passwords or current sessions, making it a low-risk hardening step.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; New passwords created after change must meet the new complexity requirements
attempt_1_commands: grep -r "dcredit" /etc/security/pwquality.conf /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/; cat /etc/security/pwquality.conf; ls -l /etc/security/pwquality.conf; grep -n "dcredit" /etc/security/pwquality.conf; sed -i 's/^# dcredit = 0/dcredit = -1/' /etc/security/pwquality.conf; grep "dcredit" /etc/security/pwquality.conf
review_feedback: The proposed plan is excellent and directly addresses the concerns raised in the previous review. Instead of using destructive 'sed' commands on existing configuration files, the plan utilizes the modern 'drop-in directory' pattern. By creating a high-priority file (99-hardening.conf), it ensures the policy is enforced consistently across the system without risking corruption of existing settings.
review_concerns: Ensure the directory /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/ exists; if it doesn't, the plan must include a command to create it (mkdir -p) before writing the file.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan follows best practices by using a drop-in configuration file in /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/ rather than modifying the main configuration file with sed. This approach is non-destructive and ensures the setting is applied correctly using the standard priority loading order of libpwquality. Setting dcredit = -1 correctly enforces the requirement for at least one digit.
qa_side_effects: Future password changes for existing users will require a digit if their current password does not meet the new complexity requirement.

title: Ensure PAM Enforces Password Requirements - Prevent the Use of Dictionary Words
triage_reason: The remediation involves enabling a standard security feature in the pam_pwquality module via a configuration file change. This is a standard STIG hardening pattern for password policies and does not impact system availability or existing user access, only affects future password changes.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; New password requirements may prevent users from choosing simple passwords during their next change
attempt_1_commands: ls /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/; ls /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/; grep -r "dictcheck" /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/; sed -i 's/^#\s*dictcheck\s*=\s*1/dictcheck = 1/' /etc/security/pwquality.conf; grep "dictcheck" /etc/security/pwquality.conf; grep "dictcheck" /etc/security/pwquality.conf

qa_verdict_reason: The plan correctly targets the pam_pwquality configuration to enable dictionary word checking. Modifying pwquality settings is a standard security hardening practice and does not impact system availability or network connectivity. While using sed to manipulate configuration files requires precision, the logic of checking for overrides in .conf.d before modifying the main config is a sound approach for managing Linux configuration hierarchies.

title: Ensure PAM Enforces Password Requirements - Minimum Different Characters

This is a standard approach for PAM configuration hardening. While sed is used, the plan specifically targets the 'difok' parameter, minimizing the risk of accidental corruption of other security settings.

title: Configure Accepting Router Advertisements on All IPv6 Interfaces
triage_reason: This is a standard sysctl hardening measure to prevent unauthorized IPv6 router advertisements. Disabling accept_ra is a common security practice and is unlikely to cause system breakage unless the system relies on IPv6 SLAAC for network connectivity in a specific environment.
triage_estimated_impact: networking affected; Potential loss of IPv6 connectivity if the network relies on Router Advertisements (RA) for address configuration (SLAAC).
attempt_1_commands: grep -r "net.ipv6.conf.all.accept_ra" /etc/sysctl.conf /etc/sysctl.d/; sysctl net.ipv6.conf.all.accept_ra; sysctl -p /etc/sysctl.d/60-disable-ipv6-accept-ra.conf; sysctl net.ipv6.conf.all.accept_ra net.ipv6.conf.default.accept_ra
attempt_3_commands: sysctl -w net.ipv6.conf.all.accept_ra=0; sysctl -w net.ipv6.conf.default.accept_ra=0; cat /proc/sys/net/ipv6/conf/all/accept_ra; cat /proc/sys/net/ipv6/conf/default/accept_ra
review_feedback: The proposed plan is excellent. It directly addresses the previous feedback by avoiding risky `sed` operations on existing configuration files and instead uses a high-priority override file in `/etc/sysctl.d/`. Including `net.ipv6.conf.default.accept_ra = 0` is a proactive measure to ensure future interfaces are also protected, and the verification step provides assurance of success.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan uses the standard and safe method of applying sysctl configurations by creating a high-priority override file in /etc/sysctl.d/. This avoids the risk of corrupting existing configuration files with regex/sed and ensures persistence. Disabling Router Advertisements is a standard hardening practice for servers that use static IPv6 addressing or managed DHCPv6.
qa_side_effects: Disabling RA may prevent the system from automatically receiving IPv6 configuration (address, gateway) if the network environment relies solely on Router Advertisements rather than static configuration or DHCPv6.
